coredata.dc:title: Construction of multi-functional optical sensing materials based on deazapurine-typed carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Linlin
Shi, Binbin
Hang, Yixiao
Xian, Chensheng
Huang, Jin
coredata.dc:description: Developing multi-functional materials is crucial to manufacture integrated devices and advanced instruments. However, the development of multi-functional properties is limited because most of the materials can only perform single-phase processing or detection. In this current study blue-emitting, amphiphilic purine-doped and amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized for the construction of multi-functional materials. These multi-functional CDs (MF-CDs) can form stable dispersions of monodisperse nanoparticles or nanoaggregates in organic/aqueous phase, which endow them with the capability to be either processed or used as detectors/sensors in a variety of solvents. The MF-CDs not only can detect pH, metal cations and the polarity of organic solvents via the change of fluorescent signals in the solution state, but also can be applied in the fields of temperature response and laser-stimulus response in a composite film. As a consequence of the architecture of the MF-CDs composite systems multi-functional characteristics can be realized successfully, which provides a new perspective to develop integrated materials at the nanoscale level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Amphiphilic
Multi-functional materials
Optical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the MF-CDs
Verifying multi-functional characteristics of the MF-CDs as nano-sensor in solution
Examining the multi-functional characteristics of MF-CDs as optical stimulus responsive and sensing materials in composite films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route of MF-CDs and their applications in optical sensing materials.

(a) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of MF-CDs (scale bar = 100 nm), Inserted Figure: images of MF-CDs in dilute solution of toluene under sunlight and 365 nm UV-light; (b) hydrodynamic diameter of MF-CDs in toluene measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS); XPS survey (c), high-resolution C 1s (d), O 1s (e) and N 1s (f) XPS spectra of MF-CDs.

(a) XRD pattern of CA, AD and MF-CDs; (b) The absorption spectrum (UV–vis), excitation spectrum (Ex) and emission spectrum (Em) of MF-CDs in toluene solution; (c) Partial enlarged view of FT-IR absorption spectra for CA (red), AD (blue) and MF-CDs (black). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

TEM images of the CDs with CA/AD reactional ratio is 1:1 in H2O (a) (scale bar = 200 nm) and in THF (d) (scale bar = 100 nm); TEM images of the CDs with CA/AD reactional ratio is 1:2 in H2O (b) (scale bar = 200 nm) and in THF (e) (scale bar = 100 nm); TEM images of the CDs with CA/AD reactional ratio is 1:4 in H2O (c) (scale bar = 200 nm) and in THF (f) (scale bar = 100 nm); DLS of the CDs with CA/AD reactional ratio is 1:1 in H2O (g) and in THF (j), 1:2 in H2O (h) and in THF (k), 1:4 in H2O (i) and in THF (l).

(a) Fluorescence evolution of MF-CDs (31.5 μg/mL) in different pH aqueous solution; Inserted figure: FIR at the maximum emission peak about 435 nm (at pH = 6, using the maximum emission peak's intensity as standard); (b) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves at 430 nm for pH = 2 (red circles), pH = 6 (black squares), pH = 12 (blue triangles); (c) Normalized emission spectrum of MF-CDs in different solvents (31.5 μg/mL); (d) Normalized absorption spectrum of MF-CDs in different solvents. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic illustration of preparation of MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and MF-CDs/PVA composite films; The normalized absorption spectra of (b) MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and (c) MF-CDs/PVA composite films with different mass fractions; The laser-light-driven deformations of MF-CDs/PVA (d) and MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL (e) composite film (1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction) at 447 nm, 100 mA, 50 mw (Scale bar = 20 cm).

The change of chromaticity coordinate (CIE 1931) for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL (a) and MF-CDs/PVA (b) composite films; The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of fluorescent composite films, I(a) MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL series and I(b) MF-CDs/PVA series; The MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL composite films doped with 8 × 10−5 mass fraction I(c) and 1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction I(d) under 365 nm UV-light; The MF-CDs/PVA composite films doped with 8 × 10−5 mass fraction I(e) and 1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction I(f) under 365 nm UV-light; (c) Fluorescence evolution of one cycle (between 40 °C and 100 °C) for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL composite film doped with 2.4 × 10−2 mass fraction (one cycle including one time increased-temperature process and one time decreased-temperature process); (d) The change of FIR for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL composite film (2.4 × 10−2 mass fraction) at 100 °C and 40 °C after 6 cycles.

head.ce:title: Construction of multi-functional optical sensing materials based on deazapurine-typed carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Synthesis, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Synthesis, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing multi-functional materials is crucial to manufacture integrated devices and advanced instruments. However, the development of multi-functional properties is limited because most of the materials can only perform single-phase processing or detection. In this current study blue-emitting, amphiphilic purine-doped and amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized for the construction of multi-functional materials. These multi-functional CDs (MF-CDs) can form stable dispersions of monodisperse nanoparticles or nanoaggregates in organic/aqueous phase, which endow them with the capability to be either processed or used as detectors/sensors in a variety of solvents. The MF-CDs not only can detect pH, metal cations and the polarity of organic solvents via the change of fluorescent signals in the solution state, but also can be applied in the fields of temperature response and laser-stimulus response in a composite film. As a consequence of the architecture of the MF-CDs composite systems multi-functional characteristics can be realized successfully, which provides a new perspective to develop integrated materials at the nanoscale level.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, CDs have attracted the attention of researchers as a consequence of their outstanding optical properties [1–3]. Compared with traditional materials [4–6], CDs have excellent luminescence properties [7,8], better water solubility [9,10], lower toxicity [11,12], higher biocompatibility [13,14], and can be readily prepared in relatively large amounts from cheap starting materials [15–17]. Therefore, CDs have a wide range of application prospects in the fields of cell imaging [18–20], reagent analysis [21] and optical detection [22,23]. In recent studies, because of higher requirements for integrated instruments, CDs have received special attention as multi-stimulus-responsive and sensing materials [24–26]. The simplest strategy to develop multi-stimuli-responsive materials is the control of the architecture of multi-functional CDs (MF-CDs) composite system [27,28]. Thus, there are three typical methods to construct the composite system. One method is chemical graft modification by the reaction with residual carboxyl or hydroxyl groups of CDs [29]. Some inorganic nanomaterials, such as silicon dioxide microspheres [30] or gold nanoparticles [31,32], can be integrated into CDs systems. In the second method, the CD is coated with polymers to realize additional functions. It is necessary to screen appropriate block copolymers to realize nanostructured assembling [33]. The third method involves doping with multiple specific groups/atoms. For instances, either nitrogen-rich or sulfur-rich CDs can be synthesized to acquire superior fluorescence performance and excellent electrical conductivity [34,35]. Nevertheless, the synthetic processes employed to form multi-functional CDs materials involve complicated procedures.

In particular, in order to obtain multi-functional characteristics, most of CDs are necessary to be processed in two phases (i.e., aqueous phase and organic phase) on account of amphiphilic property endowing their capacity to composite with other materials [36]. Specifically, amphiphilic property ensures their stable existence in multi-phase environment. And the characteristic can contribute to the construction of multi-functional composite materials [37]. However, most of CDs are only monophasic stable dispersion, which restricts their application to multi-functional composite system [38].

On the basis of these reasons, in this report, a kind of purine-doped CDs with blue-emitting, has been synthesized by solvothermal method for the construction of multi-functional composite materials (Fig. 1 ). Purine, which is widely available, cheap and non-toxic, has been selected as carbon source not only to increase fluorescent emissive intensity, but also to introduce amphiphilic property. The amphiphilic MF-CDs can exist stably in a variety of solvents. And then, the Fluorescent detection of pH, metal cations and the polarity of organic solvents can be realized to verify the multi-functional feature in solution. Meanwhile, two kinds of composite films (i.e., MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and MF-CDs/PVA) have been prepared through physical blending in organic phase or water phase respectively. The appropriate doping mass fraction of MF-CDs in composites can be adjusted, and the strong absorption of blue light region can be acquired for blue laser driven. The composites with different emissive colors can also be obtained. Simultaneously, the fluorescent temperature sensitive property could be introduced into the composites by combining with hydrogen-bonding interactions between polymeric matrix and MF-CDs. On the whole, the purine-doped CD materials can be processed both in aqueous phase and organic phase to realize multi-functionalization. This designed strategy can provide a new idea for the development of multi-stimulus-responsive optical materials.

Anhydrous citric acid (99%) used was obtained from Energy Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 6-amino-7-deazapurine (97%) was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polycaprolactone (PCL, Mw = 14,000, Mn = 10,000, PDI = 1.400), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Mw = 61,000, Mn = 40,800, PDI = 1.495) and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA, Mw = 120,000, Mn = 80,500, PDI = 1.490) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Trading Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methylbenzene (AR), trichloromethane (AR), dichloromethane (DCM, AR) and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO, AR) were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetrahydrofuran (THF, AR) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All materials were used without further purification.

Anhydrous citric acid (0.25 mmol), 6-amino-7-deazapurine (0.5 mmol) and methylbenzene (10 ml) were added into 20 ml high-pressure reactor to react 12 h at 200 °C. The reaction was then completed and cooled to room temperature, and the solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm organic filter aiming to remove unreacted raw materials and impurities. The yellow filtrate was evaporated to send out solvent. The purified MF-CDs can be stored in aqueous phase or organic phase in the dark.

PVA was dissolved in deionized water under heating conditions. THF solution of MF-CDs and PVA aqueous solution were mixed with different mass fraction. Similarly, PCL and PMMA were mixed (mPCL: mPMMA = 3 : 2) and dissolved in chloroform solvent. And the THF solution of MF-CDs was doped into the polymeric solution. The doping mass fraction of MF-CDs was from 8 × 10−5 to 2.4 × 10−2. After mixing the solutions evenly, the mixtures were poured into the Teflon plate. The solvent was evaporated 2 days at room temperature, and then they were placed into an oven at 60 °C for 6 h to remove the residue solvent.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to confirm the microstructure, and TEM images were acquired using a FEI Talos L120C microscope operating at 300 kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were obtained with Omni Nano Brookhaven instrument. The UV–vis spectra were measured using a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer, where each of the samples was placed in cuvettes (1.0 cm path length) to obtain absorption spectra. A FTIR spectrometer (ThermoFisher Nicolet is70) was used to record the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra in the 400–4000 cm−1 range. The fluorescence spectra of the MF-CDs were obtained using Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer. Absolute fluorescence quantum yields in composite films and solutions were determined via using an integrating sphere on Edinburgh Instruments FLS980. Fluorescence lifetime were measured by time correlated single photon counting mode (TCSPC) using Edinburgh Instruments FLS980, excitation light source was a 375 nm ps laser, and the frequency was 20 MHz. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using Al Kα excitation source (72 W) with a PHI 5000 VersaProbe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were done by a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer equipped with a monochromatic radiation Cu Kα operating at 40 kV and 40 mA, 2θ: 10°–80°. The temperature images of the composite films were measured by a testo 869 infrared camera. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) data were acquired by TGA2 from Mettler Toledo. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) could be measured by DSC214Polyma, NETZSCH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the MF-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped CDs have high luminous efficiency and excellent photo-stability [39]. Purine is a type of easily available ingredient as a ubiquitous substance in living things. Herein, the azathiopurine derivative (i.e., 6-amino-7-deazapurine, AD) was selected as nitrogen source. Anhydrous citric (CA) as carbon source was employed to synthesize MF-CDs by solvothermal method, and the molar ratio of AD/CA was 2:1. The products can be acquired through reacting under different temperatures from 160 °C to 200 °C. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were used to characterize the distribution and morphology of the MF-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, at the reaction temperature of 160 °C and 180 °C, the particle sizes of the products can generate uneven and large aggregates. When the reaction temperature is up to 200 °C, the products which were spherical or quasi-spherical particles, dispersed in toluene uniformly (Fig. 2 a). The result indicates that 200 °C is the optimum temperature for MF-CDs synthesis. The sizes of MF-CDs particles are mainly concentrated at 10–40 nm, and the average particle size is ca. 21 nm, which is calculated from dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement (Fig. 2b). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used for further characterization of the MF-CDs. In Fig. 2c, C, O and N elements were detected in the XPS spectra of MF-CDs, the XPS survey depicts that the content of N element is much less than C element and O element. Their relative contents are 68.75% for C element, 27.33% for O element and 3.92% for N element, respectively. The result reveals that there is a small amount of AD participating in this reaction. The high resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2d ~ Fig. 2f) confirmed the existence of C=C/C–C (284.7 eV), C–O/C–N (285.0 eV), and C=O (285.3 eV) for C 1s; C=O (532.5 eV), C–OH/C–O–C (533.3 eV) and O=C–O (533.5 eV) for O 1s; pyridinic N (396.3 eV), amino N (399.5 eV), and pyrrolic N (401.9 eV) for N 1s in the MF-CDs. These different bonding modes can also be found in raw materials (i.e., CA and AD). The characterization data prove that the new kind of MF-CDs have been synthesized successfully.

In order to further confirm the structure of the MF-CDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CA, AD and MF-CDs were measured and the results were shown in Fig. 3 a. By Comparing with that of the raw materials (i.e., CA and AD), there is a strong broad diffraction peak near 2θ = 21° in the MF-CDs curve, which suggests that the content of MF-CDs is mainly composed of amorphous carbon [40]. Meanwhile, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were employed to discuss the optical properties of the MF-CDs (Fig. 3b). The maximum emissive peak in toluene solution is ca. 402 nm and the corresponding maximum excitation wavelength is ca. 310 nm. The maximum absorption peak in solution at 268 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic C = C bonds, and the broad absorption band at about 350 nm–400 nm is ascribed to the n-π* transition of C = O [41]. In contrast to UV–vis spectra of the raw materials (Fig. S2), the absorption of MF-CDs at 350 nm–400 nm has occurred significantly enhancement, which reveals that conjugated structure has formed stemming from the MF-CDs.

Fourier Transform InfraRed (FT-IR) absorption spectra can further confirm the structure of MF-CDs. The absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 3380 cm−1 can be attributed to the N–H stretching vibration of AD and MF-CDs respectively (Fig. 3c). The absorption peak of N–H in MF-CDs has occurred significantly blue shifted, which indicates that hydrogen bonding has existed in the MF-CDs [42]. Subsequently, the absorption peaks of 1747 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 are corresponding to C=O in carboxyl-group of CA, and the absorption peaks at 1711 cm−1 and 1676 cm−1 belong to C=O in amide-group of MF-CDs [43]. The results reveals that -NH
2 of AD has reacted with -COOH of CA to generate amide bonding. In a word, all the above characterizations can fully convince that the structure of the MF-CDs has been synthesized successfully.

As mentioned above, stable and uniform dispersion and excellent optical properties both in aqueous phase and organic phase are necessary to fabricate MF-CD composites. Thus, the molar ratio of AD/CA was adjusted at the beginning of the reaction to realize optimized optical performance and dispersed stability in aqueous and organic phase. It was found that the particle size of the products increased in aqueous phase as the feeding proportion of AD increased (Fig. 4 a ~ 4c). The average diameter measured by DLS is ca. 67 nm for AD/CA = 1:1 (Fig. 4g), ca. 91 nm for AD/CA = 2:1 (Fig. 4h) and ca. 560 nm for AD/CA = 4:1 (Fig. 4i). A mass of aggregations can be observed in aqueous phase when the molar ratio of AD/CA is up to 4:1 (Fig. 4c). The products of the three feeding ratios can be evenly dispersed in THF solution (Fig. 4d ~ 4f), and the particle size of the products will be slightly reduced with the increased feeding proportion of AD (Fig. 4j ~ 4l), the size is from ca. 62 nm to ca 25 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission properties of the three feeding ratios products were compared to find the most suitable candidate for optical sensor. As shown in Fig. S3a ~ S3c and Table S1, compared to those in THF solution, the maximum fluorescence emissive peak of the all products in aqueous phase has a significant red-shift by ca. 40–50 nm. The corresponding average fluorescence lifetimes (τ
a ) of the all products in aqueous phase are longer than those in THF solution (Fig. S3d ~ S3f). These results can be attributed to the different degrees of aggregation of the products in aqueous phase and organic phase. The phenomenon is similar to some fluorescent small molecules [44]. Additionally, in the system of aqueous and organic phase, the products of MF-CDs for AD/CA = 2:1 have the highest absolute fluorescence quantum yield (AFQY) within the three feeding ratios products. It suggests that the feeding ratio AD/CA = 2:1 product can be selected as a more suitable candidate for optical sensor system in aqueous and organic phase. Therefore, they also have been screened as optical sensing materials in the following applications in solution and composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verifying multi-functional characteristics of the MF-CDs as nano-sensor in solution
ce:section.ce:para:
Sensing in solution is an important application for CDs-typed nano-sensor. Therefore, pH sensing, polar response of solvent and metal ions detection are applied to test multi-functional characteristics of the MF-CDs in solution. Firstly, pH-sensitive property was measured by photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in Fig. 5 a, PL intensity of the MF-CDs has achieved maximum emission at pH = 6. The fluorescence signal intensity of the MF-CDs will become weakening significantly when pH raises or decreases. The insert in Fig. 5a, which is the fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) versus pH, has illustrated the phenomenon that the values of FIR are normal distribution. In other word, fluorescence emissive intensity of the MF-CDs has quenched significantly in the environment of strong acid (pH = 2) or strong alkali (pH = 12). AFQYs of the MF-CDs were determined using an integrating sphere on the Edinburgh Instrument FLS980. The AFQY of the MF-CDs is 33.69% at pH = 6, while that value is 9.81% at pH = 2 and 4.29% at pH = 12. Average fluorescent lifetimes (τ
a ) can be calculated by time-resolved fluorescence decay curves. In Fig. 5b, the average fluorescent lifetime of MF-CDs at pH = 6 (2.36 ns) is significantly less than that at pH = 2 (3.51 ns) and pH = 12 (3.73 ns). For the sake of explaining these phenomena, DLS data has been acquired in aqueous phase at pH = 6, pH = 2 and pH = 12 (Fig. S4). The average particle size (d
a ) at pH = 6 is ca. 95 nm, d
a at pH = 2 is ca. 747 nm and d
a at pH = 12 is ca. 536 nm. The MF-CDs could generate aggregates in strong acid or strong alkali situation (Fig. S4). The results can be ascribed to the breakage of hydrogen bonding and amide bonding in the structure of MF-CDs [45].

Secondly, the polarity of solvent effects on fluorescent emissive signal has been investigated by dispersing the MF-CDs in different solvents. To testify the amphiphilic property of MF-CDs and their capability to be processed in solvents, the MF-CDs can be dispersed stably in H2O, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), chloroform (CHCl3), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and dichloromethane (DCM). The maximum emissive peaks of the MF-CDs in the solvents are from 400 nm to 450 nm when polarity of the solvents increases (Fig. 5c). Simultaneously, UV–vis spectra of MF-CDs shows that the absorption peaks at longer than 350 nm are strengthening with the increased polarity of solvents (Fig. 5d). It can be deduced that larger nano-aggregations have formed in stronger polar solvent according to previous literature reports [46]. By comparing the DLS data in THF, DMSO and H2O, the conclusion could be further confirmed (Fig. S5). The particle sizes of the MF-CDs dispersed in H2O and in DMSO are significantly larger than those dispersed in toluene as a monodisperse solution. The results reveal that the higher polarity of the solvent, the larger particle sizes of nano-aggregates. The aggregation at the nanoscale are similar to organic conjugated molecules [47]. In these solvents, the MF-CDs can obtain remarkable luminescence property, and their AFQYs are from 31.42% to 39.45% (Table S2). Finally, the general mechanism can be explained combine the above experimental results and the previous literature. In aprotic solvents, with the increase of solvent polarity, MF-CDs can tend to generate aggregations. In such circumstance, the influence of dipole moment on the surface electronic structure of MF-CDs will increase and their band gaps reduces, resulting in the red-shift of emissive peak [48,49]. This phenomenon is similar to the solvatochromism of organic dyes [50]. In protic solvents, the redshift of absorption and emissive spectra is mainly caused by hydrogen bonding [51,52]. The largest redshift of emissive spectra in water can be ascribed to the strongest hydrogen-bonding interaction between MF-CDs and H2O, which leads to forming larger nano-aggregates.

Detection of metal ions is a common application of CDs-typed nano-sensors due to their low toxicity, rapid response and economy [53]. Herein, eight kinds of metal ions have been tested to assess the capability. The detection based on MF-CDs has selectivity for divalent nickel ion in aqueous phase at pH = 6 (Fig. S6). In a word, the MF-CDs multi-functional applications in solution have been realized through pH sensing, detection of solvent polarity and metal-ion detecting.

According to the above experimental results, there are different degrees of aggregation for MF-CDs in aqueous solutions with different pH or in organic solvents with different polarity. Hydrogen bonding and amide bonding of MF-CDs can be broken in strong acid or base solutions. The MF-CDs will become unstable, which results in their AFQY reducing. As the polarity increasing in solution, the increased aggregation can lead to enhanced dipole-dipole interaction and their fluorescence spectra are redshifted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examining the multi-functional characteristics of MF-CDs as optical stimulus responsive and sensing materials in composite films
ce:section.ce:para: It is vital that the architecture of composite films can expand the scope of MF-CDs application and benefit for acquiring multi-functional characteristics. Based on the capability of the MF-CDs to be processed in aqueous or organic phase, two kinds of composite films (i.e., MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and MF-CDs/PVA) can be obtained (Fig. 6 a). Different doping mass fractions of MF-CDs composites, from 8 × 10−5 to 2.4 × 10−2, were prepared in order to realize excellent luminescence and absorption properties. First of all, the thermal properties of the composites were analyzed. The data of TGA for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and MF-CDs/PVA films (8 × 10−5 and 1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction) reveal that the thermal decomposition temperature (Td, 5% weight loss temperature) of the composites cannot show significant change (Fig. S7). Td of MF-CDs/PVA films with 8 × 10−5 and 1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction are 129.7 °C and 134.5 °C, respectively. And Td of MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL films with 8 × 10−5 and 1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction are 315.0 °C and 305.5 °C, respectively. Meanwhile, the obvious glass phase transition (Tg) at 29.3 °C can be observed in DSC curves for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL films with 1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction (Fig. S8). The melting temperature (Tm) of MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL films can reduce with the increase of doping mass fraction (Fig. S8). These results can indicate that the doped MF-CDs with a relatively high doping mass fraction can break the crystallinity of the polymeric matrix. The DSC curve show typical semi-crystalline solids characteristic which have both amorphous and crystalline regions. At the same time, the crystallization temperature (Tc) is almost unchanged for different doping mass fraction (Fig. S8).

Subsequently, the optical properties of the films have been discussed. When the doping mass fraction increases from 8 × 10−5 to 1.6 × 10−2, the absorption peak at ca. 430 nm can significantly enhance and attain maximum absorption (Fig. 6b and c). The result can be attributed to the increased nano-aggregates of MF-CDs as raising doping content in composites. This actional mechanism in composites is similar to what happens in solution. A mass of exciplex of in the composites can be generated as increasing doping mass fraction [54]. Further increasing doping mass fraction from 1.6 × 10−2 to 2.4 × 10−2, the absorption at ca. 430 nm decreases significantly (Fig. 6b and c). The result reveals that excessive aggregation of MF-CDs can lead to enhanced π-π stacking to be unfavorable for their optical properties [55]. Meanwhile, there has not appeared macroscopic phase separation in MF-CDs composites doped with 8 × 10−3 and 1.6 × 10−2 mass fractions (Insert Figure of Fig. 6b and c). The results suggest that the MF-CDs have excellent compatibility with the polymeric matrixes.

The maximum absorption of composites at ca. 430 nm can be prepared by doping appropriate content of the MF-CDs. It is suitable to be applied on photo-responsive materials driven by blue laser through photo-thermal conversion effect. The strip films were folded in half at about 30-degree angle, and a blue laser (MDL-Ⅲ-447nm-50mW-100mA) irradiated on the fold. As shown in Fig. 6d and e, the photos display the deformation of MF-CDs/PVA and MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL (doping with 1.6 × 10−2 mass fraction) strip at different moments (0s, 7s and 13s). The half of unfixed-strip had occurred significant circling motion by laser-driven, and the opening angle between the two halves of strip had gone from 30° to 150° within 13 s (Fig. 6e, Supporting Movie S). As control groups, the blank strips had been tested. It was unable to respond after irradiation of the blue laser within 1 min (Fig. S9). As seen from Fig. 6d and e, the opening angle of MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL strip is less than that of MF-CDs/PVA strip within 13 s.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108994

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

The mechanism of the laser-light-driven deformations can be ascribed photo-thermal effect stemming from light absorption of the composite films [56,57]. On the one hand, for a relatively high doping mass fraction (1.6 × 10−2), the MF-CDs as a photosensitizer can produce the photothermal effect. The temperature of the composite films could increase up to 34.6 °C after 10s irradiation of a blue laser (Figs. S10c and S10d). While there is no significant temperature change for the blank control group after irradiation (Figs. S10a and S10b). On the other hand, for the relatively high doping mass fraction, the temperature (34.6 °C) after 10s radiation (Fig. S10) is higher than the glass transition temperature (29.3 °C). Above the Tg temperature, the motion resistance of the polymeric molecular chain in the composites will decrease and the deformation is easy to occur. The PVA composites are easier to be driven via thermal effect after 447 nm-laser radiation due to the lower Tg (Fig. S11).

Evaluation of luminescence property is considerable for MF-CDs composite films as multi-functional materials. The different emitting color films can be acquired by adjusting different doping mass fractions (Fig. 7 a and b). The AFQYs have shown that the composites display excellent emissive performance (Table S3).

As shown in Fig. 7 I(a) and I(b), the emissive spectra of the MF-CDs composites with different doping mass fractions have been measured. At relatively low doping mass fractions (i.e. 8 × 10−5 and 4 × 10−4), the maximum emissive peaks of MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and MF-CDs/PVA are located at 410 nm and 405 nm, which are similar to the situation in dilute solution. The result reveals that there is few nano-aggregates generated at the doping mass fractions. Further increasing the doping mass fraction from 8 × 10−4 to 8 × 10−3, the exciplex peaks at 518 nm for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and at 485 nm for MF-CDs/PVA greatly increased. The result shows that the aggregates content increases in the composites [55]. Upon increasing the doping mass fraction to 1.6 × 10−2 and 2.4 × 10−2, the strong emissive peaks at 518 nm for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and 485 nm for MF-CDs/PVA have shown high levels of aggregates. These suggest that aggregates content of MF-CDs plays a leading role in the composites. By comparing with MF-CDs/PVA films, there is a wider range of CIE values for MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL films (Table S3), and the emitting colors can be controlled from dark blue to cold white (Fig. 7a:I(c), 5a:I(d) and Fig. 7b:I(f), 5b:I(e)). In comparison to MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL composite system, a stronger hydrogen bonding effect in PVA composites can inhibit the formation of MF-CDs aggregates at high doping mass fractions. Because the structural units of PVA are rich in hydroxyl groups, which provides more opportunities to form hydrogen bonding in the composites [43].

The hydrogen bonding in CDs structure can be weakened by heating, which makes fluorescence emission reduce or quench [58]. In order to acquire a wide responsive temperature range, MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL composites can became a suitable candidate for temperature sensing due to the MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL composites having higher Tg than that of MF-CDs/PVA composites. As shown in Fig. 7c, when the temperature increases from 40 °C to 100 °C, the fluorescence intensity of composites doping with 2.4 × 10−2 mass fraction will gradually reduce. And fluorescence intensity will recover when the temperature cools down from 100 °C to 40 °C. Taking the maximum emissive peak at 40 °C as a reference standard, the fluorescence quenching rate at 100 °C is about 60%. Meanwhile, one cycle includes a heating process and a cooling process, and the change of fluorescence intensity in one cycle has been monitored to evaluate the stability of temperature sensing. The corresponding fluorescence intensity at the same temperature is almost the same in a cycle (Fig. 7c). The result indicates that the composite system has high stability and sensitivity as temperature sensor. Heating and cooling procedure were implemented six times between 40 °C and 100 °C in order to further confirm the stability of temperature sensing. After six cycles, the excellent repeatability and reliability for fluorescent temperature sensing have been shown in FIR versus temperature image (Fig. 7d). Therefore, MF-CDs multi-functional characteristics applied in the field of optical stimulus responsive and sensing materials can be examined by the above results in the composites.

In a word, the generation of MF-CDS aggregates in the composites is closely related to the doping content. At the relatively low doping mass fractions, the emissive and absorptive spectra of MF-CDs are similar to those in solution. As the doping mass fraction increased in composites, the nano-aggregates of MF-CDs will increase and their absorption at the region of ca. 440 nm enhances at a relatively high doping mass fraction. After the films being irradiated by the 447 nm-laser, such characteristic ensures that obvious photothermal effect can be produced to drive the movement of the composite strips.

In summary, a new type of deazapurine-doped and amphiphilic CDs has been synthesized to construct multi-functional materials via processing in organic or aqueous phase. As a kind of optical nano-sensor material, the MF-CDs can respond to pH, polar solvents and metal ions in solution. In aqueous phase detection, the maximum AFQY of MF-CDs can be obtained when pH is 6, and the emissive spectra will occur redshift significantly when the polarity of solvent increases. They also have good selectivity sensing for divalent nickel ion. Meanwhile, MF-CDs/PMMA-PCL and MF-CDs/PVA composites can be prepared by physical blending in organic or aqueous phase respectively. They can be applied on fast response to 447 nm laser. By monitoring fluorescence signals of the MF-CDs composites, they can be used as temperature sensor between 40 and 100° Celsius. These results have revealed that the MF-CDs can be applied in the field of multi-functional and optical responsive materials with environmentally friendly, simple-processing, quick-response, highly sensitive and repeatable. The materials constructed strategy could give a new perspective to develop optical multi-stimulus-responsive materials for integrated equipment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Nanjing Tech University (Start-up Grant 39837133), SICAM Fellowship from Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials and “Jiangsu innovative and entrepreneurial talent plan”, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21805133), the Natural Science Research Projects in Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (Grant Nos. 18KJB150020) and Postgraduate Research&Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (Grant Nos. KYCX20_1051).
